chunks: Module III Bottom Up Parsing Bottom up Parsing 2 Types • Operator precedence parser • LR Parsers Bottom up Parsing • Bottom up parsing is a process reducing a string w to start symbol of the grammar. • At each reduction step, a specific substring matching the body of the production is replaced by the non terminal at the head of that production. E  E + T | T T T * F | F F  (E)| id Construct “id * id” Bottom up Parsing • The key decision during bottom up parsing are about • When to reduce • What production to apply • Right most derivation in reverse order can be obtained by handle pruning. • Handle – non terminal Handle • A handle of a string is a substring that matches the right side of a production and whose reduction to the non terminal on the left side of the production represents one step along the reverse of a right most derivation. Shift Reduce Parsing • Shift Reduce Parser│Compiler Design│Part# 15│Learn CSE Malayalam Shift Reduce Parsing • A general form of bottom up syntax analysis is called shift reduce parsing. • Generally used by LR parsers. • Operator precedence parser is also a form of shift reduce parsing. Shift Reduce Parsing 1.Input Buffer: This stores the string

alysis is called shift reduce parsing. • Generally used by LR parsers. • Operator precedence parser is also a form of shift reduce parsing. Shift Reduce Parsing 1.Input Buffer: This stores the string or sequence of tokens that needs to be parsed. 2.Stack: The parser uses a stack to keep track of which symbols or parts of the parse it has already processed. As it processes the input, symbols are pushed onto and popped off the stack. 3.Parsing Table: Similar to a predictive parser, a parsing table helps the parser decide what action to take next. Shift Reduce Parsing – Before Parsing • A $ is placed at the stack top • A $ is append to the right end of input w$ Shift Reduce Parsing – After Parsing • The stack contains $ along with the start symbol • The input buffer contains the symbol $ Shift Reduce Parsing – Basic Operations 1. Shift : Shift symbols from input buffer onto the stack 2. Reduce : If the handle appears on top of the stack, then its reduction by using appropriate production rule is done. 3. Accept : Successful parsing is done 4. Error: The parser can neither perform shift action nor reduce action, and not even accept action. Shift Reduce Parsing – Example E  E + T | T T

rule is done. 3. Accept : Successful parsing is done 4. Error: The parser can neither perform shift action nor reduce action, and not even accept action. Shift Reduce Parsing – Example E  E + T | T T T * F | F F  (E)| id Construct “id * id” Shift Reduce Parsing – Parsing Table Stack Input Buffer Action $ id*id $ Shift $id *id$ Reduce F  id $F *id$ Reduce T  F $T *id$ Shift $T* id$ Shift $T*id $ Reduce F  id $T*F $ Reduce T  T * F $T $ Reduce E  T $E $ Accept Conflicts • Every shift reduce parser can reach a configuration in which a parser knowing the entire stack contents and the next input symbol. • Can’t decide whether to shift or to reduce called as shift reduce conflicts • Can’t decide which of the several reductions to be made called as the reduce reduce conflicts. Advantages • Shift-reduce parsing is efficient and can handle a wide range of context-free grammars. • It can parse a large variety of programming languages and is widely used in practice. • It is capable of handling both left- and right-recursive grammars, which can be important in parsing certain programming languages. • The parse table generated for shift-reduce parsing is typically small, which makes the

dling both left- and right-recursive grammars, which can be important in parsing certain programming languages. • The parse table generated for shift-reduce parsing is typically small, which makes the parser efficient in terms of memory usage. Disadvantages • Shift-reduce parsing has a limited lookahead, which means that it may miss some syntax errors that require a larger lookahead. • It may also generate false-positive shift-reduce conflicts, which can require additional manual intervention to resolve. • Shift-reduce parsers may have difficulty in parsing ambiguous grammars, where there are multiple possible parse trees for a given input sequence. • In some cases, the parse tree generated by shift-reduce parsing may be more complex than other parsing techniques. Example • Consider the grammar S –> ( L ) | a L –> L , S | S Perform Shift Reduce parsing for input string “( a, ( a, a ) ) “.
